Notice Of Petition And Petition To Compel Arbitration And For A Stay Of Dlse Proceedings
Off Calendar Apr-17-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PETITIONER CRESTWOOD BEHAVIORAL HEALTH, INC.'S Petition To Compel Arbitration And For A Stay Of Dlse Proceedings

Petitioners Crestwood Behavioral Health, Inc.'s petition to compel arbitration is granted and the DLSE proceedings shall be stayed pending the arbitration of plaintiff DeEveria Lacy's claims. This order shall constitute the Court's statement of decision on the petition pursuant to Code Civ. Proc. 1291.

There is no dispute that Plaintiff signed a Dispute Resolution & Arbitration Policy, which requires binding arbitration of all employment-related legal disputes. Contrary to Crestwood's argument, that Policy does not clearly and unmistakably delegate the threshold issues of arbitrability and enforceability to the arbitrator. The arbitration clause provides that the parties waive all rights to a civil court action for disputes covered by the policy, including without limitation disputes "arising out of or relating to the interpretation or application of this Policy." That clause does not mention the threshold issues of enforceability or validity. (Cf. Aanderud v. Superior Court (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 880, 892 [delegation clause by which the parties agreed to arbitrate "all disputes, claims and controversies arising out of or relating to . . . the interpretation, validity, or enforceability of this Agreement, including the determination of the scope or applicability of this Section 5 [the 'Arbitration of Disputes' section]" provided clear and unmistakable evidence that the parties intended to delegate threshold issue of arbitrability to the arbitrator]; compare also Malone v. Superior Court (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1551, 1560.])

Plaintiff's argument that defendant failed to provide her with a notice to arbitrate is meritless. Plaintiff, as the party asserting employment-related claims, was required by the Policy to make a written demand for arbitration. The California Arbitration Act expressly authorizes Crestwood to file a petition to compel arbitration. (Code Civ. Proc. 1281.2.) While Plaintiff correctly asserts that the Labor Commissioner has jurisdiction over her claims, waiver of the right to a Berman hearing does not necessarily render an arbitration agreement unenforceable or unconscionable per se, so long as "the arbitral scheme at issue provides employees with an accessible and affordable process for resolving wage disputes." (Sonic-Calabasas A, Inc. v. Moreno (2013) 57 Cal.4th 1109, 1146-48.) Plaintiff has not submitted any evidence that the Policy is procedurally unconscionable. (See Performance Team Freight Systems, Inc. v. Aleman (2015) 241 Cal.App.4th 1233, 1247 ["Absent any evidence, we cannot just assume there was procedural unconscionability."].) Nor does she contend that the Policy is substantively unconscionable, or that the arbitral forum provided under the Policy is not accessible or affordable. (Cf. Subcontracting Concepts (CT), LLC v. De Melo (Cal. Ct. App. Apr. 10, 2019) 2019 WL 1552684.)

Because the parties' agreement to submit all of Plaintiff's claims to an arbitral forum is enforceable, the proceedings before the Labor Commissioner shall be stayed pending the conclusion of the arbitration proceedings in order to avoid frustration of the main object of the parties' agreement: a quick and streamlined resolution of their disputes governed by the Policy. (See Preston v. Ferrer (2008) 552 U.S. 346, 357 [arbitration following the Labor Commissioner's decision "would likely be long delayed, in contravention of Congress' intent 'to move the parties to an arbitrable dispute out of court and into arbitration as quickly and easily as possible.'"]).

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan Schulman; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/EPS)
